We've all done this thousands of times and pretty much had it backfire. Everyone does this. In an effort to try and soften what comes next, you say these types of phrases because you hope that your friend will tell you in a couple sentences that you're not that bad. And while this is close to an accusations audit, it falls short because of a small but critical mistake. The good news here is that your own gut instincts, your emotional intelligence is correctly picking up their negative emotions or dynamics at a present. The two millimeter tweak is to get away from the use of a denial as an approach. Denying the negative implants it and actually gives it credibility. Their emotional reaction is that if you're denying it, it must be true. Let me give you an example. While I was with the FBI, I was on a surveillance in a residential area of New Jersey at about eight o'clock in the morning. I actually crossed the intersection for me was the school crossing with a school crossing guard. I figured she was going to get nervous about me being there because it might look like I was stalking the kids. I didn't want her to call the police and have some marked cars come up and blow my position so I went over to her, showed her my FBI ID and then made the huge mistake, the denial. I said, I don't want you to think I'm here stalking the kids. And guess what? Four minutes later I was surrounded by two marked police cars with their lights on and I was ordered out of my car to be searched. What should I have said to her? I should have said I'm sure it looks like I'm some sort of crazy weirdo while I was showing her my ID. She would have said no. I appreciate you coming over though and it would have been fine. To fully grasp how this technique works, look no further than defense lawyers. In their opening statement they list everything their client is accused of and all the weaknesses of their case. It's called taking the sting out. This concept is very, very difficult for people to wrap their heads around but not because it's complicated but because of the way you react the first time you think about it. What you're going to do is list every terrible thing your counterpart could say about you. You're just bringing the negatives to light. You're not self-loathing and you're not trying to get their sympathy. This trick works by disarming your opponent and reducing the negativity which brings both parties to a safe zone of empathy. What this also shows is a fearlessness on your part. It actually makes them have much more trust towards you. Before you go into an interaction list out all the things you'd want to deny. All the things you know they shouldn't be thinking about you. Their fears, biases, misconceptions, resentments and unfair perceptions. Then call them out in advance. Your brain will react with horror the first time you think about doing this. You're going to be afraid they'll say, I wasn't thinking that but now I am. That's not going to happen. But you won't get over your fear until you actually try it out. Or are lucky enough to see it being done and watch it make barriers disappear. And the first time you do it, well if you get silence that means you're on the right track. Keep going. When they give you some form of hey don't be so hard on yourself. Or their voice changes and they say some form of okay so what is it you want me to do for you? You're in good shape. Anytime you get even a whiff of their fears in the conversation go back to this tool and dig them back out and diffuse them. One of our coaches, Derek, was recently coaching a client in a divorce proceeding. Her husband, the client was a woman, her husband wanted her to pay him a half a million dollars. She was a successful one of the two. She knew that the laws were going to require her to pay not more than $35,000 but was prepared to go to $50,000 just to get it over with. Our coach Derek had her list out all the negative things her soon to be ex-husband would say. It was a 17 item long list. She sat down with the husband and all the lawyers involved and laid out the list. And you know what happened? Instead of the half a million dollars her husband wanted or the $50,000 she was prepared to pay she settled for just under $35,000. Her lawyer was shocked. When you see the kind of magic that can be worked with the accusations on it you'll love it.
